title: Southeast Asia & Oceania
subtitle: Temple cities, island stone, and the deepest-time painting tradition on earth.
intro: From the sandstone temple-mountains of Angkor and Borobudur to basalt moai on Easter Island and rock shelters in northern Australia where painting has gone on for 20,000 years.

works.title: Borobudur
works.desc: A stone mandala you walk. Climbing its nine terraces takes you through 2,672 relief panels and 504 Buddhas to the empty stupa at the top.

works.title: Prambanan
works.desc: The Hindu counterpart to Borobudur a few miles away — a cluster of tall, sharp-edged temples to the Trimurti, walls carved with the Ramayana.

works.title: Angkor Wat
works.desc: The largest religious monument on earth. Built as a Hindu temple to Vishnu, converted to Buddhist use, and never fully abandoned.

works.title: Bagan
works.desc: More than 2,000 Buddhist temples and stupas on a plain by the Irrawaddy. At dawn hot air balloons drift over a landscape of brick towers.

works.title: Wat Phra Kaew
works.desc: The royal temple inside the Grand Palace, home to the Emerald Buddha — carved from a single block of jade, robed differently for each season.

works.title: Nan Madol
works.desc: A city of 92 artificial islets built from hexagonal basalt logs, laid across a lagoon. The "Venice of the Pacific" — and a place no one has fully explained.

works.title: Moai
works.desc: Nearly 900 stone figures, carved and moved miles from the quarry. They face inland — watching over the villages, not out to sea.

works.title: Asmat Carving
works.desc: Bisj poles and shields carved from mangrove with sharpened bone. Each pole commemorates a death and demands it be avenged.

works.title: Māori Wharenui
works.desc: A carved meeting house represents an ancestor's body — ridgepole as spine, rafters as ribs. Every panel tells a story in whakairo carving.

works.title: Tā Moko
works.desc: Tattoos chiseled (not punctured) into the skin, carrying genealogy and rank. Each design is unique to the person who wears it.

works.title: Ubirr Rock Art
works.meta: Aboriginal Australian · up to 20,000 years old · Kakadu
works.desc: One of the oldest continuous art traditions on earth. Later paintings show fish anatomy so precisely that species are still identifiable.

works.title: Mỹ Sơn
works.desc: Brick Hindu temples in a jungle valley. Seventy stood before American bombs in 1969; twenty survive, still slowly giving up their secrets.

works.title: Ayutthaya
works.desc: The Thai capital before the Burmese sacked it in 1767. Brick prangs lean at odd angles around the old moated island.

works.title: Sukhothai
works.desc: Serene standing Buddhas in the "walking" pose invented here — one hand raised, the other swinging at the hip, fabric flowing forward.

works.title: Wayang Kulit
works.desc: Flat leather shadow puppets of the Ramayana and Mahabharata, manipulated against a backlit screen. A traditional performance runs all night.

works.title: Đông Sơn Culture
works.desc: Bronze drums cast with spiral patterns and concentric bird-men. Traded across mainland Southeast Asia — a pre-Indic shared world.

works.title: Bayon
works.desc: The last great temple of Angkor — 200 serene faces carved into its towers, likely portraits of Jayavarman VII himself, watching from every direction.

works.title: Preah Vihear
works.desc: A cliff-top temple on the Thai border — 800 meters of causeway climbing to a sanctuary looking out over the plain. Thailand and Cambodia fought over its ownership in 2011.

works.title: Balinese Temples
works.desc: Every village has at least three: for the ancestors, for daily life, for the dead. Split gates, black palm-fiber roofs, statues wrapped in checkered cloth.

works.title: Indigenous Australian Art
works.desc: Bradshaw paintings (Gwion Gwion) in the Kimberley, possibly 20,000 years old — elongated figures painted with pigment that has bonded permanently with the rock surface.

works.title: Uluru
works.desc: A sandstone monolith rising 1,100 feet out of the center of the continent. For the Aṉangu it is the physical record of ancestral beings' creation journeys.

works.title: Angkor Thom
works.desc: The last capital of the Khmer Empire — nine square kilometers walled in laterite. At its heart, the Bayon; at its gates, giants pulling serpents in cosmic tug-of-war.

works.title: Ananda Temple
works.desc: A cruciform white temple with four standing Buddhas, each eighteen feet tall. From a distance the gold umbrella on its stupa is the last thing to disappear.

works.title: Besakih Temple
works.desc: The Mother Temple of Bali — 23 linked sanctuaries up the slope of Mount Agung. When Agung erupted in 1963, lava stopped meters from its gates.

works.title: Banaue Rice Terraces
works.desc: Stone-walled terraces climbing the mountainside. Built without wheeled carts, draft animals, or cement. If unwrapped they'd circle half the globe.

works.title: Plain of Jars
works.desc: Thousands of carved stone jars scattered across a plateau. Probably funerary; ground-truth research has been slow because the plain is still littered with unexploded American bombs.

works.title: Shwedagon Pagoda
works.desc: A gilded stupa 325 feet tall, covered in gold leaf and crowned with 4,000 diamonds. Said to enshrine hairs of the Buddha.

works.title: Banteay Srei
works.desc: "Citadel of Women" — a small temple of pink sandstone carved with almost impossibly fine reliefs. Malraux tried to steal four figures in 1923 and was arrested.

works.title: Tongkonan
works.desc: Ancestral houses with boat-shaped roofs soaring up at both ends. Each is built facing north, aligned to the cosmos of the Torajan dead.

works.title: Hei-tiki
works.desc: Nephrite pendants of a human figure worn around the neck, passed down through generations. Each is named; each gains mana from the people who wear it.

works.title: Rongorongo
works.desc: Glyphs carved onto wooden tablets — one of the few independent writing systems in human history. Still undeciphered; most tablets were burned by missionaries.

works.title: Candi Sukuh
works.desc: A Hindu temple built as a truncated stone pyramid — closer to Maya than to Angkor. Its reliefs are frank about fertility; the statues at the gate tell you what they're for.

works.title: Moon of Pejeng
works.meta: Dong Son tradition · c. 300 BCE · Bali
works.desc: The largest bronze kettledrum in the world — almost two meters across. In Balinese legend it's the fallen wheel of a chariot of the moon.

works.title: Wat Rong Khun (White Temple)
works.meta: Contemporary Thai Buddhist · Chalermchai Kositpipat · 1997 onward · Chiang Rai
works.desc: An entire temple sculpted white, glittering with shards of mirror. The artist intends it to take centuries to complete; hands rise from hell at the entrance.
